notes.note: Quintus Cicero had governed the rich province of Asia since 61 BC; his command, meant to be brief, was prorogued a third year to 59 BC, the occasion of this letter of advice. Cicero blames himself for failing to secure a successor.
Roman magistrates who, after their year of office in Rome, were assigned provinces to govern; here the praetors at Rome whose support Cicero had hoped would secure Quintus's recall.

The publicani were companies of Roman businessmen who bought, by auction from the censors, the right to collect a province's taxes for profit. In Asia they were politically powerful and a constant source of friction between governor and provincials.

notes.anchor: the highest command for three years in Asia
notes.note: Asia, the Roman province organised from the Attalid kingdom of Pergamum after 129 BC, covered western Asia Minor and was the wealthiest of Rome's eastern provinces --- proverbially a place where a governor could enrich himself.
The contrast frames the ideal Roman governor as a protector of the provincials rather than a plunderer, the central theme of the whole letter.

Tubero, the senior legate, was an author of annales (year-by-year history); Cicero jokes that he can find models to imitate in his own pages. (This L. Aelius Tubero is distinct from the Stoic Q. Aelius Tubero of De Re Publica.)
Legati were senior senatorial deputies assigned to assist a provincial governor; men of standing who shared in the dignity and the responsibility of the command.
A governor's quaestor --- his financial officer --- was assigned not by choice but by sortition, so Quintus had no say in the man's character and had to manage him as he found him.
The cohors praetoria was the governor's personal staff and entourage --- friends, attendants, and hangers-on --- for whose conduct, Cicero stresses, the governor was held publicly answerable.
The signet ring (anulus) sealed official documents and so authorised them; Cicero urges that it witness Quintus's own will, not be lent out as a tool of others' designs.
Lictors were the official attendants who walked before a magistrate with imperium, carrying the fasces and clearing his path; their conduct reflected directly on the governor.
The fasces --- bundled rods, with axes added outside Rome --- were the lictors' emblems of a magistrate's power to compel and to execute; Cicero wants them to signal dignity rather than menace.

The Via Appia, Italy's most prestigious highway running south from Rome, stands as the benchmark of orderly travel: Quintus's slaves should behave on the Asian roads as if still on a public road in Italy.
Tralles was a wealthy city of inland Asia; Formiae a coastal town in Latium where Cicero owned a villa. Pairing them tells the household to treat a provincial city no differently from home soil.

notes.anchor: a Mysian or rather a Phrygian
notes.note: Mysians and Phrygians were Roman bywords for ignoble, contemptible origin; Cicero dismisses the complainant Paconius as not even a proper Greek but the lowest sort of Anatolian.

C. Octavius, praetor 61 and governor of Macedonia (60 BC), is held up as the model of a judge whose mildness was made possible by underlying severity; he was the father of the future emperor Augustus.

Beneficiaries of the dictator Sulla's settlement (d. 78 BC), who had seized property and lands by force; Octavius compelled them to give back what they had taken, a politically delicate act of justice.
Xenophon's Cyropaedia portrays Cyrus the Great not as history but as an idealised model of the just ruler; it was a touchstone of Greek and Roman thinking on good government.
Scipio Aemilianus (Africanus the younger, 185-129 BC), conqueror of Carthage and the central figure of Cicero's De Re Publica, was famously devoted to Xenophon's Cyropaedia.
Samos, the island off the Ionian coast, was reckoned the noblest city of Ionia; Cicero credits Quintus with restoring it and Halicarnassus in Caria from ruin.
Mysia, a region of north-western Asia Minor in the province, was plagued by banditry that Quintus is praised for suppressing.

Aediles at Rome funded the public games from their own pockets and routinely pressed provincial cities for contributions; Quintus's edict barring such levies for the games freed Asia of a heavy exaction.
Sums in sesterces (HS), the standard Roman unit of account; 200,000 sesterces was a substantial sum, the contribution one Roman noble claimed Quintus's edict had cost him.

notes.anchor: that prince of talent and learning
notes.note: Plato (c. 428-348 BC), whose Republic argues that states flourish only when philosophers rule or rulers philosophise --- the 'philosopher-king' doctrine Cicero applies to Quintus's learned governance of Asia.

notes.anchor: consecrated and placed among the gods
notes.note: Greek cities honoured benefactors and Roman governors with cult --- temples, statues, festivals --- treating them almost as gods; Cicero takes such honours to Quintus as genuine and worth preserving.
Cicero names the central dilemma: the tax-farming companies were the order to which he owed his political base, yet indulging them ruined the provincials a just governor was bound to protect.
Provincials paid vectigal (tribute) to Rome; Cicero argues the Greeks had long paid tribute under their own kings and to one another, so Roman tax should sting less than they pretend.

The lex censoria was the standard contract by which the censors at Rome let out the tax-collecting concessions; Cicero hopes the publicani will settle their pacts by convenience rather than the letter of that harsh instrument.
Cicero casts the whole province of Asia as a vast theatre whose verdict on Quintus's conduct will carry all the way back to Rome --- a metaphor for the public scrutiny of a governor's reputation.
